meta.compliance_deadline_basis: 24 months from circular issuance (10 March 2026) — applicable to all Other Financial Institutions including Microfinance Banks

overall_rating.summary_paragraph: Sunrise Microfinance Bank Ltd faces a HIGH overall compliance risk against the CBN Baseline Standards for Automated AML Solutions. While the institution has implemented partial AML automation covering customer due diligence and sanctions/PEP screening, critical functional gaps exist in transaction monitoring, fraud detection, case management, and regulatory reporting — all of which are mandatory under the Circular. The institution operates across multiple states with 10,000–100,000 customers, processes 1,000–50,000 transactions monthly, and offers FX/remittance products that elevate cross-border ML/TF risk exposure. PEP exposure further compounds screening and monitoring obligations. Of the 12 CBN Baseline Standards, 7 are rated Critical Gap and 3 are rated Gap Identified, with only 2 standards currently compliant. Governance maturity is weak (30% score), with no documented AML governance framework, change control, alert SLAs, vendor management policy, or data retention policy in place. Internal audit covers AML annually, but the absence of enterprise case management, transaction monitoring, and automated regulatory reporting creates significant examination risk. The institution has 23 months remaining to achieve full compliance by March 2028, and must submit a credible implementation roadmap to the CBN by 10 June 2026 — now 8 weeks away.
overall_rating.sector_context_note: Microfinance Banks are classified as Other Financial Institutions under the CBN AML/CFT/CPF Regulations, 2022, and are subject to the full scope of the Money Laundering (Prevention and Prohibition) Act, 2022 and the Terrorism (Prevention and Prohibition) Act, 2022. MFBs with multi-state operations, material customer bases, and FX/remittance products face elevated ML/TF risk and are expected to deploy proportionate automated AML controls. The CBN has signalled heightened supervisory focus on MFBs offering cross-border payment services, particularly in relation to sanctions screening, transaction monitoring, and STR quality. Sunrise MFB's current partial automation and governance gaps place the institution at material risk of regulatory censure, administrative sanctions under the CBN AML, CFT and CPF (Administrative Sanctions) Regulations, 2023, and reputational damage in the event of a financial crime incident or adverse examination finding.

scorecard.aml_system_status_label: Partial — CDD/KYC and sanctions screening automated; transaction monitoring, fraud detection, case management, and regulatory reporting not covered
scorecard.governance_score_label: 30% (3 of 10 governance controls in place: MLRO appointed, Board AML/CFT policy approved, BVN/NIN integration operational)
scorecard.internal_audit_label: AML controls reviewed annually by internal audit
scorecard.risk_factors_label: PEP exposure, Cross-border FX/remittance products, Multi-state operations, 10K–100K customer base, 1K–50K monthly transactions
scorecard.regulatory_context_box: CBN Circular BSD/DIR/PUB/LAB/019/002 issued 10 March 2026. Roadmap submission deadline: 10 June 2026 (8 weeks remaining). Full compliance deadline for MFBs: March 2028 (23 months remaining). Governing legislation: Money Laundering (Prevention and Prohibition) Act, 2022; Terrorism (Prevention and Prohibition) Act, 2022; CBN AML/CFT/CPF Regulations, 2022; CBN AML, CFT and CPF (Administrative Sanctions) Regulations, 2023. Regulatory authority: Central Bank of Nigeria. FIU: Nigerian Financial Intelligence Unit.

gap_analysis_intro: This gap analysis assesses Sunrise Microfinance Bank Ltd's current AML/CFT/CPF control environment against each of the 12 CBN Baseline Standards (§5.1–§5.12). The institution has implemented partial automation covering customer due diligence, KYC/KYB, and sanctions/PEP screening, and has appointed an MLRO and obtained Board approval for an AML/CFT policy. However, critical functional gaps exist in transaction monitoring (§5.5), fraud detection (§5.6), case management (§5.7), regulatory reporting (§5.8), system integration (§5.10), and user interface/customisation (§5.12). Governance weaknesses (§5.9) are pervasive: no documented AML governance framework, no change control process, no alert SLAs, no vendor management policy, no data retention policy, and no evidence of staff training records. The institution's exposure to PEP clients and cross-border FX/remittance products elevates the materiality of these gaps, particularly in relation to sanctions screening quality, transaction monitoring for cross-border typologies, and STR generation. Each standard below is rated Compliant, Gap Identified, or Critical Gap, with findings tailored to Sunrise MFB's specific risk profile, product mix, and operational footprint. All findings reference the specific CBN standard section(s) they relate to, and required actions are sequenced to support the institution's roadmap submission and compliance timeline.

standards.title: AML Solution (Functional Footprint)
standards.finding: Sunrise MFB has deployed a partial AML solution covering customer identification/verification, customer risk assessment/profiling, and sanctions/PEP screening. However, the solution does not cover transaction monitoring for ML/TF/PF risk, fraud monitoring, case management and investigation, or regulatory/internal reporting — all of which are mandatory functional components under §5.1. The Circular requires an integrated automated AML platform commensurate with the institution's size, complexity, risk profile, and transaction volumes. With 10,000–100,000 customers, 1,000–50,000 monthly transactions, multi-state operations, and FX/remittance products, Sunrise MFB's risk profile demands a comprehensive solution. The absence of transaction monitoring and case management creates a material gap in the institution's ability to detect, investigate, and report suspicious activity. The institution has indicated intent to automate transaction monitoring and case management ahead of the 10 June 2026 roadmap deadline, which is appropriate. However, the current partial footprint does not meet the §5.1 requirement for an integrated platform with appropriate availability, resilience, and disaster recovery. The institution must document its implementation approach (upgrade existing solution vs. replace vs. hybrid), name an implementation owner, and justify the chosen architecture against its risk profile in the roadmap submission.
standards.required_action: Complete functional gap closure by deploying or procuring modules for transaction monitoring, fraud detection, case management, and regulatory reporting. Document the implementation approach, timeline, and resource allocation in the CBN roadmap submission due 10 June 2026. Ensure the solution architecture supports integration with core banking, KYC repositories, and external data sources (BVN/NIN, sanctions lists, adverse media). Establish disaster recovery and business continuity arrangements with documented RTO/RPO derived from a Business Impact Analysis. Notify the CBN of all AML solutions deployed, as required by §5.2.

standards.title: Customer Due Diligence (CDD), KYC, and KYB
standards.finding: Sunrise MFB has implemented automated or semi-automated CDD/EDD/KYC/KYB processes, including automated risk profiling and integration with BVN/NIN systems. This meets the core requirements of §5.2. The institution's governance data confirms BVN/NIN integration is operational, which is a mandatory control for Nigerian financial institutions. However, the Circular requires continuous KYC-to-transaction-monitoring data synchronisation and a single investigator interface linking KYC data to transaction history and case outcomes. Because the institution does not yet have transaction monitoring or case management in place (see §5.1, §5.5, §5.7), the full §5.2 requirement for end-to-end CDD-to-investigation workflow cannot yet be satisfied. Once transaction monitoring and case management are deployed, the institution must ensure that KYC data flows in real-time or near-real-time to the monitoring engine, and that investigators can view a unified customer profile spanning KYC attributes, transaction history, alerts, and case outcomes in a single interface. The institution must also notify the CBN of all AML solutions deployed, including the CDD/KYC/KYB platform currently in use.
standards.required_action: Maintain current CDD/KYC/KYB automation and BVN/NIN integration. Once transaction monitoring and case management are deployed (per §5.1, §5.5, §5.7), configure bidirectional data synchronisation between the KYC repository and the monitoring/case management platforms to enable continuous risk profiling updates and a unified investigator interface. Notify the CBN of the CDD/KYC/KYB solution currently in use, and update the notification when transaction monitoring and case management are commissioned. Document the data flow architecture and synchronisation frequency in the AML governance framework (see §5.9).

standards.title: Sanction Lists & PEP Screening
standards.finding: Sunrise MFB has implemented sanctions and PEP screening as part of its partial AML solution. This is a positive control. However, §5.3 requires real-time or near-real-time screening against domestic and global sanctions lists, PEP registers, and adverse media, with AI/fuzzy-name matching, institution-specific internal watchlists, automated transaction interdiction or blocking on confirmed matches, and comprehensive screening logs as evidence. The institution must conduct periodic and continuous screening in addition to onboarding screening. Given Sunrise MFB's PEP exposure (identified as a risk factor) and cross-border FX/remittance products, the quality and coverage of sanctions/PEP screening is material. The institution has not provided evidence of: (a) continuous screening (i.e., re-screening existing customers against updated lists); (b) adverse media screening; (c) automated transaction interdiction on confirmed matches; or (d) institution-specific internal watchlists (e.g., for customers with prior suspicious activity or adverse audit findings). The absence of transaction monitoring (§5.5) means that even if a PEP or sanctioned entity is flagged at onboarding, there is no automated mechanism to monitor their subsequent transaction behaviour for ML/TF/PF risk. This is a material gap for an institution offering FX/remittance products, which are high-risk channels for sanctions evasion and PF typologies.
standards.required_action: Enhance the existing sanctions/PEP screening solution to support: (a) continuous screening (daily or weekly re-screening of the entire customer base against updated sanctions lists, PEP registers, and adverse media); (b) adverse media screening using AI/NLP tools; (c) automated transaction interdiction or blocking on confirmed matches, with documented escalation to the MLRO; (d) institution-specific internal watchlists for high-risk customers, including PEPs, customers with prior STRs, and customers flagged by internal audit or external regulators. Configure fuzzy-name matching with documented thresholds and false positive management. Maintain comprehensive screening logs (customer ID, screening date, lists checked, match results, investigator disposition, timestamp) as evidence for CBN examination. Given the institution's PEP exposure and cross-border FX products, ensure that PEP screening covers domestic PEPs, foreign PEPs, and close associates/family members, and that cross-border sanctions lists (OFAC, UN, EU, UK) are included in addition to Nigerian lists.

standards.title: Risk Assessment
standards.finding: Sunrise MFB has implemented automated customer risk profiling as part of its CDD/KYC solution, which partially satisfies §5.4. However, the Circular requires configurable automated risk assessment tied to the institution's documented risk appetite, with dynamic customer risk profile updates in response to new data, behavioural changes, and external factors, and enterprise-level ML/TF/PF risk identification. The institution has not provided evidence of: (a) a documented enterprise-level ML/TF/PF risk assessment; (b) dynamic risk profile updates triggered by transaction behaviour (which requires transaction monitoring — see §5.5); (c) risk assessment governance, including periodic review and recalibration of risk scoring models; or (d) documented risk appetite statements approved by the Board. The absence of transaction monitoring means that customer risk profiles are static (based on onboarding data only) and do not reflect actual transaction behaviour, which is a core requirement of §5.4. The institution's exposure to PEPs and cross-border FX products means that risk assessment must incorporate behavioural analytics and external risk factors (e.g., adverse media, sanctions list changes, high-risk jurisdiction exposure). The institution has indicated that AI/ML is not currently in use, which is acceptable, but if AI/ML is introduced in future (e.g., for transaction monitoring or risk scoring), §5.4 requires documented governance with human oversight and explainability.
standards.required_action: Conduct and document an enterprise-level ML/TF/PF risk assessment covering all products, channels, customer segments, and geographies. Obtain Board approval for the risk assessment and a documented risk appetite statement. Configure the customer risk assessment engine to support dynamic risk profile updates triggered by: (a) transaction monitoring alerts (once §5.5 is implemented); (b) sanctions/PEP screening hits; (c) adverse media; (d) changes in customer profile (e.g., PEP status, high-risk jurisdiction exposure); and (e) external risk factors (e.g., FATF grey-listing of a customer's country of origin). Establish a governance process for periodic review and recalibration of risk scoring models (at least annually, or more frequently if material risk factors change). Document risk scoring methodology, weighting of risk factors, and thresholds for high/medium/low risk classification. Retain evidence of risk assessment outcomes and resulting configuration changes for CBN examination.

standards.title: Transaction Monitoring & Risk-Based Analyses
standards.finding: Sunrise MFB does not currently have automated transaction monitoring in place. This is a Critical Gap. Section 5.5 is the most requirement-dense standard in the Circular, with 13 sub-requirements covering risk-based transaction monitoring using predictive analytics and anomaly detection, pre-emptive alerts for high-risk scenarios, comprehensive rule-based monitoring using CDD/KYC/KYB attributes (not raw transaction data alone), related-party mapping and network analysis, AI/ML explainability (if applicable), consolidated alert views, annual independent model validation, documented false positive/negative thresholds, SLA for high-risk alert review, and strict governance for automated alert closure (with CBN notification if used). The absence of transaction monitoring means that Sunrise MFB has no automated capability to detect suspicious transaction patterns, structuring, rapid movement of funds, cross-border typologies, or other ML/TF/PF red flags. This is a material control failure for an institution processing 1,000–50,000 transactions monthly across retail deposits, credit/loans, and FX/remittance products. The institution's exposure to cross-border FX and PEP clients further elevates the risk. Without transaction monitoring, the institution is reliant on manual review by branch staff or ad-hoc investigations, which is explicitly non-compliant with the Circular's automation requirement. The institution has indicated intent to automate transaction monitoring ahead of the 10 June 2026 roadmap deadline, which is appropriate and urgent. The Circular prohibits standalone or batch-feed transaction monitoring for institutions rated High or Above Average risk; while Sunrise MFB is rated Medium risk by the CBN, the institution's multi-state operations, FX/remittance products, and PEP exposure mean that transaction monitoring must be integrated with the KYC repository and customer risk profiles, with real-time or near-real-time data feeds from core banking. The institution must also establish documented SLAs for high-risk alert review, and if automated alert closure is contemplated, must notify the CBN and implement strict governance controls (Maker-Checker, audit trail, periodic review of auto-closed alerts).
standards.required_action: Deploy automated transaction monitoring as a matter of urgency. The solution must support: (a) risk-based monitoring using predictive analytics and anomaly detection; (b) pre-emptive alerts for high-risk scenarios (e.g., structuring, rapid movement of funds, cross-border transfers to high-risk jurisdictions, PEP transactions above threshold); (c) comprehensive rule-based monitoring using CDD/KYC/KYB attributes (customer risk rating, PEP status, occupation, expected transaction profile) in addition to raw transaction data; (d) related-party mapping and network analysis to detect layering and integration typologies; (e) consolidated alert views linking alerts to customer profiles, prior alerts, and case history; and (f) integration with core banking and KYC repositories via real-time or near-real-time data feeds (not standalone batch files). Configure monitoring rules and scenarios to address cross-border FX typologies (e.g., trade-based money laundering, sanctions evasion, remittance structuring) and PEP-related risks (e.g., unexplained wealth, politically exposed transactions). Establish documented SLAs for high-risk alert review (e.g., same-day review for critical alerts, 48-hour review for high-priority alerts). Document false positive and false negative thresholds and conduct annual independent model validation. If automated alert closure is used, notify the CBN and implement Maker-Checker controls with periodic management review of auto-closed alerts. Retain comprehensive alert logs (alert ID, customer ID, scenario triggered, alert date, investigator, disposition, rationale, timestamp) as evidence for CBN examination.

standards.title: Fraud Monitoring and Detection
standards.finding: Sunrise MFB does not currently have automated fraud monitoring in place. This is a Critical Gap. Section 5.6 requires automated fraud monitoring in real-time or near-real-time across all channels (cards, e-channels, deposits, lending). The institution operates mobile app and USSD channels in addition to its branch network, and offers retail deposits, credit/loans, and FX/remittance products — all of which are fraud-prone. The absence of automated fraud monitoring creates material operational risk, customer protection risk, and reputational risk. While §5.6 is distinct from AML/CFT obligations, the Circular requires that fraud monitoring be integrated or tightly coordinated with AML workflows, with clear segregation of fraud and AML rules. The institution must support updatable fraud rules, unified or tightly integrated AML/fraud workflows, fraud registry interfaces, and historical fraud trend analysis. The institution's multi-state operations and digital channels (mobile app, USSD) elevate fraud risk, particularly in relation to account takeover, SIM swap, and social engineering attacks. The absence of fraud monitoring also undermines the institution's ability to detect predicate offences for money laundering (e.g., proceeds of fraud being layered through the institution's accounts). The Circular does not require institutions rated Medium risk to demonstrate a unified financial crime architecture (shared data lake, coordinated case management) at this stage, but the institution should consider this as a medium-term objective to enhance operational efficiency and reduce total cost of ownership.
standards.required_action: Deploy automated fraud monitoring covering all channels (branch, mobile app, USSD) and all products (retail deposits, credit/loans, FX/remittance). The solution must support: (a) real-time or near-real-time fraud detection using rule-based and behavioural analytics; (b) updatable fraud rules to respond to emerging typologies; (c) logical separation of fraud and AML rules if deployed on the same platform, or tight integration if deployed on separate platforms; (d) fraud registry interfaces to check customers against known fraudsters; and (e) historical fraud trend analysis to inform rule tuning and risk assessment. Configure fraud rules to address channel-specific risks (e.g., card-not-present fraud, account takeover, SIM swap, social engineering, loan application fraud). Establish a fraud case management workflow with clear escalation paths and coordination with AML case management (see §5.7). Retain fraud alert logs and case outcomes as evidence for internal audit and CBN examination. Consider a medium-term roadmap towards unified financial crime architecture (shared data lake, coordinated AML/fraud case management) to enhance operational efficiency and reduce false positives.

standards.title: Case Management
standards.finding: Sunrise MFB does not currently have Enterprise Case Management (ECM) in place. This is a Critical Gap. Section 5.7 requires ECM with: automated case creation, assignment, prioritisation and tracking; role-based workflows with Maker-Checker functionality and escalation paths; full tamper-proof audit trails (timestamps, users, decisions, rationale); and management reporting on case volumes, ageing, and outcomes. The absence of ECM means that the institution has no structured process for investigating alerts (once transaction monitoring is deployed — see §5.5), documenting investigation outcomes, escalating cases to the MLRO, or generating STRs. This creates material compliance risk and examination risk. The institution has indicated intent to automate case management ahead of the 10 June 2026 roadmap deadline, which is appropriate and urgent. ECM is a foundational control that underpins the institution's ability to demonstrate compliance with the MLPPA, TPPA, and CBN AML/CFT/CPF Regulations. The Circular requires that cases be investigated promptly (with documented SLAs for high-risk cases) and that closed cases be periodically reviewed for patterns and control weaknesses. The institution must also ensure that case management supports Maker-Checker functionality (i.e., the investigator who creates a case cannot also close it without independent review) and that all case actions are logged in a tamper-proof audit trail. The absence of case management also undermines the institution's ability to generate management information (MI) for the CCO, senior management, ECO, and Board on case volumes, ageing, outcomes, and STR quality.
standards.required_action: Deploy Enterprise Case Management (ECM) as a matter of urgency. The solution must support: (a) automated case creation from transaction monitoring alerts, sanctions/PEP screening hits, and manual referrals; (b) role-based workflows with automated assignment, prioritisation (based on risk rating and alert type), and tracking; (c) Maker-Checker functionality (investigator creates case, supervisor reviews and approves closure); (d) escalation paths to the MLRO for high-risk cases and STR decisions; (e) tamper-proof audit trail capturing all case actions (timestamps, user identity, decisions, rationale, supporting evidence); and (f) management reporting on case volumes, ageing, outcomes, STR generation, and false positive rates. Configure case workflows to align with the institution's documented AML governance framework (see §5.9) and alert SLAs (see §5.5). Establish a process for periodic review of closed cases (at least quarterly) to identify patterns, control weaknesses, and opportunities for rule tuning. Retain case records and audit trails as evidence for internal audit and CBN examination. Ensure that ECM integrates with transaction monitoring (§5.5), sanctions/PEP screening (§5.3), and regulatory reporting (§5.8) to enable end-to-end workflow from alert generation to STR submission.

standards.title: Reporting
standards.finding: Sunrise MFB does not currently have automated or semi-automated regulatory reporting in place. This is a Critical Gap. Section 5.8 requires automated or semi-automated generation of: STRs (Suspicious Transaction Reports), SARs, CTRs (Currency Transaction Reports), FTRs (Foreign Currency Transaction Reports), and other CBN-prescribed returns. The institution must also generate internal MI reporting to the CCO, senior management, ECO, and Board. The absence of automated reporting means that the institution is reliant on manual processes (e.g., email-based STR submission, spreadsheet-based CTR/FTR compilation), which is explicitly non-compliant with the Circular. Manual reporting creates material compliance risk (missed reporting deadlines, incomplete or inaccurate reports, lack of audit trail) and operational risk (staff error, data integrity issues). The institution's FX/remittance products trigger mandatory CTR and FTR obligations, and the institution's PEP exposure and cross-border FX risk profile mean that STR generation is a material control. The Circular requires that external reporting be made only to authorities with a lawful mandate (CBN, NFIU, EFCC, ICPC), and that internal MI reporting be provided to the CCO, senior management, ECO, and Board on a regular basis (at least quarterly). The institution must ensure that regulatory reporting integrates with case management (§5.7) so that STRs are generated directly from closed cases, with full audit trail and supporting evidence attached.
standards.required_action: Deploy automated or semi-automated regulatory reporting covering: (a) STRs (generated from case management system upon MLRO approval); (b) CTRs (automated extraction of cash transactions above threshold from core banking); (c) FTRs (automated extraction of foreign currency transactions above threshold from core banking); and (d) other CBN-prescribed returns (e.g., quarterly AML/CFT compliance returns). Configure STR generation to pull data directly from case management (§5.7), including customer details, transaction details, investigation narrative, supporting evidence, and MLRO approval timestamp. Establish a process for internal MI reporting to the CCO, senior management, ECO, and Board covering: case volumes, alert volumes, STR volumes, CTR/FTR volumes, false positive rates, case ageing, and key risk indicators. Ensure that all regulatory reports are submitted via the prescribed channels (e.g., goAML portal for STRs to NFIU, CBN returns portal for compliance returns) and that submission confirmations are retained as evidence. Implement role-based access controls to ensure that only authorised users (MLRO, CCO, designated compliance officers) can generate and submit regulatory reports. Retain comprehensive reporting logs (report type, generation date, submission date, recipient, user identity, timestamp) as evidence for CBN examination.

standards.title: Audit and Governance
standards.finding: Sunrise MFB has material governance weaknesses. The institution has appointed an MLRO and obtained Board approval for an AML/CFT policy, which are positive controls. Internal audit covers AML controls annually, which satisfies the minimum requirement. However, the institution has not established a documented AML governance framework, change control process, model governance process, alert SLAs, vendor management policy, data retention policy, or staff training records. This represents a governance score of 30% (3 of 10 controls in place), which is rated Weak. Section 5.9 requires a comprehensive, tamper-proof and immutable audit trail of all system and user activities (configuration changes, access events, alert dispositions, report generation), with records including user identity, date, timestamp, and nature of activity. The institution must also establish a documented governance framework covering roles, configuration management, change control, model validation, access rights, and incident handling. The absence of a governance framework means that the institution has no documented process for: (a) approving and documenting AML system configuration changes (e.g., new monitoring rules, threshold changes, user access grants); (b) validating and recalibrating risk models and monitoring scenarios (required annually under §5.5); (c) managing vendor relationships and ensuring vendor compliance with CBN requirements; (d) retaining and destroying AML data in accordance with legal and regulatory obligations; or (e) training AML/CFT/CPF staff and maintaining training records. These are material control weaknesses that create compliance risk, operational risk, and examination risk. The institution must also ensure that the AML platform (once fully deployed) generates a tamper-proof audit trail supporting forensic investigation, with verifiable linkages across customer data, transactions, alerts, user actions, and regulatory returns.
standards.required_action: Establish and document a comprehensive AML governance framework covering: (a) roles and responsibilities (MLRO, CCO, AML analysts, IT, internal audit, Board); (b) configuration management (process for approving, documenting, and testing AML system configuration changes); (c) change control (Maker-Checker approval for rule changes, threshold changes, user access changes); (d) model governance (annual independent validation of risk models, monitoring scenarios, and fraud rules, with documented validation methodology and outcomes); (e) alert SLAs (documented timeframes for alert review, investigation, and escalation, differentiated by risk rating); (f) vendor management (policy for selecting, contracting, and monitoring AML technology vendors, including due diligence, SLA management, and exit planning); (g) data retention (policy for retaining AML data — customer records, transaction records, alerts, cases, reports — for the period required by law, currently 5 years under the MLPPA); (h) data destruction (secure deletion of AML data at end of retention period); (i) access rights (role-based access controls, periodic access reviews, segregation of duties); and (j) incident handling (process for responding to AML system failures, data breaches, or control weaknesses). Obtain Board approval for the governance framework. Implement a staff training programme covering AML/CFT/CPF obligations, system usage, typologies, and regulatory updates, and maintain training records (staff name, training date, topics covered, assessment results) as evidence for CBN examination. Ensure that the AML platform (once fully deployed) generates a comprehensive, tamper-proof audit trail of all system and user activities, and that audit logs are retained for the full data retention period. Conduct periodic reviews of audit logs (at least quarterly) to detect unauthorised access, configuration changes, or control weaknesses.

standards.title: System Integration & Scalability
standards.finding: Sunrise MFB has not provided evidence of secure, bidirectional real-time or near-real-time integration between its partial AML solution (CDD/KYC, sanctions/PEP screening) and core banking, or of integration architecture to support transaction monitoring, fraud detection, case management, and regulatory reporting (which are not yet deployed). This is a Critical Gap. Section 5.10 requires secure, bidirectional real-time or near-real-time integration with core banking, KYC systems, and other relevant applications via standards-based APIs. The institution must support standardised data exchange formats, legacy integration, and scalability to handle growing volumes without performance degradation. The Circular explicitly prohibits High or Above Average risk institutions from relying solely on standalone transaction feeds; while Sunrise MFB is rated Medium risk, the institution's multi-state operations, 10,000–100,000 customer base, 1,000–50,000 monthly transactions, and FX/remittance products mean that integration quality is material. The absence of real-time integration means that: (a) customer risk profiles in the AML system may be stale (not reflecting recent KYC updates or transaction behaviour); (b) transaction monitoring (once deployed) will operate on delayed or incomplete data; (c) sanctions/PEP screening may not trigger real-time transaction interdiction; and (d) case management and regulatory reporting will require manual data extraction and reconciliation. The institution must also ensure that integration architecture supports scalability (i.e., the AML platform can handle transaction volume growth without performance degradation) and that shared services arrangements (if any) are approved by the CBN in advance.
standards.required_action: Design and document an integration architecture for the AML platform covering: (a) bidirectional real-time or near-real-time data exchange between core banking and the AML platform (customer data, account data, transaction data, KYC updates, risk profile updates); (b) integration between the KYC repository and the AML platform to support continuous risk profiling and unified investigator views; (c) integration between sanctions/PEP screening and core banking to support real-time transaction interdiction on confirmed matches; (d) integration between transaction monitoring, case management, and regulatory reporting to enable end-to-end workflow from alert generation to STR submission; and (e) integration with external data sources (BVN/NIN, sanctions lists, adverse media, fraud registries). Use standards-based APIs (REST, SOAP, or equivalent) and standardised data exchange formats (JSON, XML, or equivalent). Ensure that integration supports legacy systems (if applicable) and that the architecture is scalable to handle transaction volume growth (e.g., 2x or 3x current volumes) without performance degradation. Document integration architecture, data flows, API specifications, and error handling procedures in the AML governance framework (see §5.9). If shared services arrangements are contemplated (e.g., outsourcing AML monitoring to a third-party provider), obtain prior CBN approval and ensure that the arrangement is documented in a formal SLA with clear accountability, data protection, and audit rights.

standards.title: Security & Data Protection
standards.finding: Sunrise MFB has not provided evidence of a documented data security architecture for its AML platform. Section 5.11 requires encryption of data at rest, in use, and in transit; role-based access controls; Multi-Factor Authentication (MFA); compliance with the Nigeria Data Protection Act (NDPA) and Nigerian data sovereignty requirements; and defined RTO/RPO from a Business Impact Analysis. The institution has not documented a data retention policy (see §5.9), which is a prerequisite for §5.11 compliance. The absence of a documented security architecture creates material data protection risk, cyber risk, and regulatory risk (potential breach of NDPA). The institution must ensure that AML data (customer records, transaction records, alerts, cases, reports) is encrypted at rest (e.g., database encryption, disk encryption) and in transit (e.g., TLS/SSL for API calls, encrypted file transfers). Role-based access controls must ensure that only authorised users can access AML data, and that access is logged in the audit trail (see §5.9). MFA must be implemented for all AML system users to prevent unauthorised access. The institution must also ensure that AML data is stored and processed in Nigeria (or in a jurisdiction approved by the CBN) to comply with Nigerian data sovereignty requirements, and that data retention and destruction policies comply with the MLPPA (5-year retention period) and NDPA. The institution must conduct a Business Impact Analysis to define RTO (Recovery Time Objective) and RPO (Recovery Point Objective) for the AML platform, and implement disaster recovery and business continuity arrangements to meet these objectives.
standards.required_action: Document and implement a data security architecture for the AML platform covering: (a) encryption of data at rest (database encryption, disk encryption, or equivalent); (b) encryption of data in transit (TLS/SSL for API calls, encrypted file transfers, or equivalent); (c) encryption of data in use (memory encryption, or equivalent, if processing sensitive data in cloud environments); (d) role-based access controls (RBAC) with documented roles, permissions, and access approval workflows; (e) Multi-Factor Authentication (MFA) for all AML system users; (f) compliance with the Nigeria Data Protection Act (NDPA), including data subject rights (access, rectification, erasure), consent management, and breach notification; (g) Nigerian data sovereignty (AML data stored and processed in Nigeria, or in a jurisdiction approved by the CBN); (h) data retention (5-year retention period for AML records under the MLPPA, with documented retention schedule and storage arrangements); (i) data destruction (secure deletion of AML data at end of retention period, with documented destruction procedures and certificates of destruction); and (j) disaster recovery and business continuity (documented RTO/RPO derived from a Business Impact Analysis, with tested backup and recovery procedures). Obtain Board approval for the data security architecture and data retention/destruction policy. Conduct periodic security assessments (at least annually) and penetration testing to validate the effectiveness of security controls. Retain evidence of security assessments, MFA implementation, access reviews, and disaster recovery tests for CBN examination.

standards.title: User Interface & Customisation
standards.finding: Sunrise MFB does not currently have real-time or near-real-time dashboards for key AML/CFT/CPF metrics, alerts, and case management. This is a Critical Gap. Section 5.12 requires real-time or near-real-time dashboards for key AML/CFT/CPF metrics, alerts, and case management, with support for multi-entity, multi-currency, and multi-jurisdiction configurations where relevant, and configurable workflows, escalation paths, and alert filters under documented governance. The absence of dashboards means that the MLRO, CCO, and senior management have no real-time visibility into AML control performance, alert volumes, case ageing, or STR generation. This undermines the institution's ability to manage AML risk proactively and respond to emerging threats. The institution must also ensure that the AML platform (once fully deployed) supports configurable workflows, escalation paths, and alert filters, with all configuration changes documented and approved under the governance framework (see §5.9). The institution operates across multiple states and offers FX/remittance products, which may involve multi-currency transactions; the AML platform must support multi-currency configurations and reporting. The institution must also maintain documented processes for updating rules, scenarios, and thresholds, with Maker-Checker approval and audit trail (see §5.9).
standards.required_action: Deploy real-time or near-real-time dashboards for the MLRO, CCO, senior management, and Board covering: (a) key AML/CFT/CPF metrics (alert volumes, case volumes, STR volumes, CTR/FTR volumes, false positive rates, case ageing, high-risk customer counts, sanctions/PEP screening hit rates); (b) alert management (open alerts by risk rating, alert ageing, alerts by scenario, alerts by investigator); (c) case management (open cases by risk rating, case ageing, cases by outcome, STR generation rate); and (d) regulatory reporting (STR submission status, CTR/FTR submission status, overdue reports). Configure dashboards to support drill-down into underlying data (e.g., click on alert count to view list of alerts, click on case to view case details). Ensure that the AML platform supports multi-currency configurations (for FX/remittance products) and multi-entity configurations (if the institution expands to subsidiary or branch structures in future). Implement configurable workflows, escalation paths, and alert filters under documented governance (see §5.9), with Maker-Checker approval for all configuration changes. Maintain documented processes for updating rules, scenarios, and thresholds, including: (a) change request and approval workflow; (b) impact assessment (expected change in alert volumes, false positive rates); (c) testing and validation (back-testing against historical data); (d) implementation and rollback procedures; and (e) post-implementation review (actual vs. expected outcomes). Retain evidence of all configuration changes in the audit trail (see §5.9).

governance_assessment.intro: Sunrise MFB's AML governance maturity is Weak, with a score of 30% (3 of 10 governance controls in place). The institution has appointed an MLRO, obtained Board approval for an AML/CFT policy, and integrated with BVN/NIN systems — all of which are positive foundational controls. However, the institution has not established a documented AML governance framework, change control process, model governance process, alert SLAs, vendor management policy, data retention policy, or staff training programme. These are material control weaknesses that create compliance risk, operational risk, and examination risk. The absence of a governance framework means that the institution has no documented process for managing AML system configuration, validating risk models, managing vendor relationships, retaining and destroying AML data, or training AML staff. The institution must address these governance gaps as a matter of urgency, in parallel with deploying transaction monitoring, fraud detection, case management, and regulatory reporting. The governance framework must be documented, approved by the Board, and operationalised before the institution submits its roadmap to the CBN on 10 June 2026. Internal audit covers AML controls annually, which is adequate, but the scope of internal audit must be expanded to cover the new AML platform components (transaction monitoring, fraud detection, case management, regulatory reporting) once they are deployed.

items.status: Yes — Amara Okonkwo is designated Chief Compliance Officer

items.control: Documented AML governance framework (roles, configuration management, change control, model validation, access rights, incident handling)
Change control process for AML system configuration (Maker-Checker approval, testing, audit trail)
items.status: No — change control process not documented

items.control: Model governance (annual independent validation of risk models, monitoring scenarios, fraud rules)
items.status: No — model governance process not documented

items.control: Alert SLAs (documented timeframes for alert review, investigation, escalation, differentiated by risk rating)
Vendor management policy (due diligence, SLA management, exit planning for AML technology vendors)
items.status: No — vendor management policy not documented

items.control: Data retention and destruction policy (5-year retention under MLPPA, secure deletion at end of retention period)
items.status: No — data retention policy not documented

items.control: AML/CFT/CPF staff training programme with documented training records

priority_actions.title: Complete and Submit the CBN Implementation Roadmap by 10 June 2026
priority_actions.body: The CBN roadmap submission is the institution's most urgent regulatory obligation. The roadmap template spans 12 sections and requires the institution to document: (a) current AML system status and functional gaps; (b) target state architecture (integrated AML platform covering transaction monitoring, fraud detection, case management, regulatory reporting); (c) implementation approach (upgrade existing solution vs. replace vs. hybrid); (d) implementation timeline with milestones and deliverables; (e) resource allocation (budget, staff, vendors); (f) governance arrangements (MLRO, project owner, steering committee); (g) risk mitigation (interim controls during implementation, fallback plans); and (h) compliance deadline (March 2028 for MFBs). The roadmap must be credible, detailed, and approved by the Board before submission. Sunrise MFB has 8 weeks remaining to complete the roadmap. OPEX Consulting can complete the roadmap on the institution's behalf, leveraging this gap assessment report and the institution's input on implementation approach, budget, and timeline. The roadmap must reference this gap assessment report as evidence of the institution's current state, and must address all 12 CBN Baseline Standards (§5.1–§5.12) with specific actions, timelines, and accountability. Failure to submit a credible roadmap by 10 June 2026 will result in regulatory censure and may trigger supervisory intervention.

priority_actions.title: Select and Commission an Integrated AML Platform Covering Transaction Monitoring, Fraud Detection, Case Management, and Regulatory Reporting
priority_actions.deadline_label: Target: Q3 2026 (vendor selection and contracting); Q4 2026 — Q2 2027 (implementation and go-live)
priority_actions.body: Sunrise MFB must deploy an integrated AML platform to close the critical functional gaps in transaction monitoring (§5.5), fraud detection (§5.6), case management (§5.7), and regulatory reporting (§5.8). The institution has indicated intent to automate these functions ahead of the roadmap deadline, which is appropriate. The institution must decide whether to: (a) upgrade its existing CDD/KYC/sanctions screening solution to add transaction monitoring, fraud detection, case management, and reporting modules (if the vendor offers these); (b) replace the existing solution with a new integrated platform; or (c) adopt a hybrid approach (retain existing CDD/KYC solution, procure separate transaction monitoring/fraud/case management platform, integrate via APIs). The decision must be documented in the roadmap submission and justified against the institution's risk profile, budget, and implementation timeline. RegTech365 RegPort is a proven integrated AML platform covering transaction monitoring, sanctions/PEP screening, customer risk assessment, and regulatory reporting, and is designed for Nigerian financial institutions. RegGuard provides real-time fraud monitoring, and RegComply provides enterprise case management with Maker-Checker controls and tamper-proof audit trails. OPEX Consulting can support the vendor selection process, including requirements definition, vendor evaluation, proof-of-concept testing, and contract negotiation. The institution must ensure that the selected platform supports: (a) real-time or near-real-time integration with core banking and KYC repositories; (b) configurable monitoring rules and scenarios addressing cross-border FX typologies and PEP-related risks; (c) AI/fuzzy-name matching for sanctions/PEP screening; (d) related-party mapping and network analysis; (e) Maker-Checker workflows and tamper-proof audit trails; (f) automated STR/CTR/FTR generation; and (g) real-time dashboards for the MLRO, CCO, and senior management. The institution must also ensure that the platform is scalable to handle transaction volume growth and that the vendor provides training, support, and annual model validation services.

priority_actions.title: Establish and Document a Comprehensive AML Governance Framework
priority_actions.deadline_label: Target: Q2 2026 (before roadmap submission); operationalise Q3 2026 (in parallel with platform deployment)
priority_actions.body: Sunrise MFB must establish and document a comprehensive AML governance framework covering roles and responsibilities, configuration management, change control, model governance, alert SLAs, vendor management, data retention and destruction, access rights, and incident handling (§5.9). The governance framework is a prerequisite for credible roadmap submission and for operationalising the new AML platform. The framework must be approved by the Board and must assign clear accountability for AML system ownership, configuration management, and compliance monitoring. The institution must document: (a) roles and responsibilities (MLRO, CCO, AML analysts, IT, internal audit, Board); (b) configuration management (process for approving, documenting, and testing AML system configuration changes, including new monitoring rules, threshold changes, user access grants); (c) change control (Maker-Checker approval for all configuration changes, with documented change requests, impact assessments, testing results, and post-implementation reviews); (d) model governance (annual independent validation of risk models, monitoring scenarios, and fraud rules, with documented validation methodology, findings, and remediation actions); (e) alert SLAs (documented timeframes for alert review, investigation, and escalation, differentiated by risk rating — e.g., same-day review for critical alerts, 48-hour review for high-priority alerts); (f) vendor management (policy for selecting, contracting, and monitoring AML technology vendors, including due diligence, SLA management, performance reviews, and exit planning); (g) data retention (5-year retention period for AML records under the MLPPA, with documented retention schedule, storage arrangements, and access controls); (h) data destruction (secure deletion of AML data at end of retention period, with documented destruction procedures and certificates of destruction); (i) access rights (role-based access controls, periodic access reviews, segregation of duties between investigators and approvers); and (j) incident handling (process for responding to AML system failures, data breaches, control weaknesses, or adverse examination findings). The governance framework must be operationalised in parallel with AML platform deployment, with documented evidence of governance activities (change requests, model validation reports, access reviews, incident logs) retained for CBN examination.

priority_actions.title: Enhance Sanctions and PEP Screening to Support Continuous Screening, Adverse Media, Transaction Interdiction, and Internal Watchlists
priority_actions.deadline_label: Target: Q3 2026 (in parallel with transaction monitoring deployment)
priority_actions.body: Sunrise MFB has implemented sanctions and PEP screening, which is a positive control. However, the institution must enhance the screening solution to support: (a) continuous screening (daily or weekly re-screening of the entire customer base against updated sanctions lists, PEP registers, and adverse media); (b) adverse media screening using AI/NLP tools to detect negative news about customers (e.g., corruption allegations, fraud convictions, sanctions violations); (c) automated transaction interdiction or blocking on confirmed sanctions/PEP matches, with documented escalation to the MLRO; and (d) institution-specific internal watchlists for high-risk customers, including PEPs, customers with prior STRs, customers flagged by internal audit or external regulators, and customers with adverse media hits (§5.3). The institution's PEP exposure and cross-border FX/remittance products elevate the materiality of sanctions/PEP screening. The institution must ensure that PEP screening covers domestic PEPs (Nigerian politically exposed persons), foreign PEPs (PEPs from other jurisdictions), and close associates and family members of PEPs. The institution must also ensure that cross-border sanctions lists (OFAC, UN, EU, UK) are included in addition to Nigerian sanctions lists, and that sanctions screening is conducted in real-time at the point of transaction (not just at onboarding). The institution must configure fuzzy-name matching with documented thresholds (e.g., 80% match threshold for high-risk names, 90% match threshold for medium-risk names) and false positive management (documented process for reviewing and dispositioning screening hits, with audit trail). The institution must maintain comprehensive screening logs (customer ID, screening date, lists checked, match results, investigator disposition, rationale, timestamp) as evidence for CBN examination. OPEX Consulting can support the configuration and tuning of sanctions/PEP screening rules, and RegTech365 RegPort includes AI-powered sanctions/PEP screening with adverse media monitoring.

priority_actions.title: Implement a Staff Training Programme for AML/CFT/CPF with Documented Training Records
priority_actions.deadline_label: Target: Q3 2026 (initial training for AML platform users); ongoing (annual refresher training and new hire onboarding)
priority_actions.body: Sunrise MFB must implement a staff training programme covering AML/CFT/CPF obligations, AML system usage, ML/TF/PF typologies, sanctions and PEP screening, transaction monitoring, case investigation, STR generation, and regulatory updates (§5.9). The institution must maintain documented training records (staff name, training date, topics covered, assessment results, trainer identity) as evidence for CBN examination. Training is a cross-cutting obligation under Section 6 of the Circular and is a prerequisite for effective AML control operation. The institution must ensure that: (a) all AML/CFT/CPF staff (MLRO, CCO, AML analysts, compliance officers, branch staff with AML responsibilities) receive initial training before the AML platform goes live; (b) training covers system usage (how to review alerts, investigate cases, generate STRs, configure rules), typologies (common ML/TF/PF red flags, cross-border FX typologies, PEP-related risks, fraud typologies), and regulatory obligations (MLPPA, TPPA, CBN AML/CFT/CPF Regulations, CBN Baseline Standards); (c) training is delivered by qualified trainers (internal or external) with documented expertise in AML/CFT/CPF; (d) training includes assessments (quizzes, case studies, or practical exercises) to validate staff understanding; (e) training records are retained for the full data retention period (5 years under the MLPPA); and (f) annual refresher training is provided to all AML/CFT/CPF staff, with additional training when new regulations, typologies, or system features are introduced. RegTech365 RegLearn provides AML, data protection, governance, and regulatory compliance training tailored to Nigerian financial institutions, with online modules, assessments, and automated training record management. OPEX Consulting can also deliver bespoke AML training workshops for Sunrise MFB staff, covering system usage, typologies, and regulatory obligations.

roadmap.intro: This implementation roadmap provides a phased approach for Sunrise Microfinance Bank Ltd to achieve full compliance with the CBN Baseline Standards for Automated AML Solutions by the March 2028 deadline. The roadmap is structured in four phases spanning 23 months, from now (April 2026) through March 2028. Phase 1 focuses on foundation-building and roadmap submission (now — June 2026). Phase 2 focuses on governance and architecture design (July — September 2026). Phase 3 focuses on AML platform deployment and go-live (October 2026 — June 2027). Phase 4 focuses on assurance, examination readiness, and embedding (July 2027 — March 2028). Each phase includes specific objectives, key deliverables, and CBN standards addressed. The roadmap assumes that the institution will select and commission an integrated AML platform in Q3 2026, with implementation and go-live in Q4 2026 — Q2 2027. The roadmap also assumes that the institution will establish a documented AML governance framework in Q2 2026 (before roadmap submission) and operationalise the framework in parallel with platform deployment. The institution must assign a dedicated implementation owner (project manager or programme manager) with clear accountability for roadmap delivery, and must establish a steering committee chaired by the MLRO or CCO with representation from IT, operations, internal audit, and senior management. The institution must also allocate sufficient budget and resources (staff, vendors, technology) to deliver the roadmap on time and to the required quality standard. OPEX Consulting can provide programme management, vendor selection, implementation support, and assurance services to de-risk roadmap delivery.

phases.title: Foundation & Submission
phases.objectives: Complete and submit the CBN implementation roadmap by 10 June 2026. Establish foundational governance controls (AML governance framework, change control process, data retention policy). Select and contract an integrated AML platform vendor. Assign a dedicated implementation owner and establish a steering committee. Obtain Board approval for the roadmap, governance framework, and implementation budget.
phases.key_deliverables: CBN implementation roadmap (12 sections, Board-approved, submitted by 10 June 2026). Documented AML governance framework covering roles, configuration management, change control, model governance, alert SLAs, vendor management, data retention, access rights, and incident handling (Board-approved). Vendor selection report documenting requirements definition, vendor evaluation, proof-of-concept results, and contract terms for the integrated AML platform. Implementation project charter naming the implementation owner, steering committee members, project scope, timeline, budget, and success criteria. Board resolution approving the roadmap, governance framework, and implementation budget.
phases.standards_addressed: All standards (§5.1–§5.12) are addressed in the roadmap submission. §5.9 (governance framework, change control, data retention policy). §5.1 (vendor selection and contracting for integrated AML platform).

phases.title: Governance & Architecture
phases.objectives: Design the integration architecture for the AML platform, covering bidirectional real-time or near-real-time data exchange with core banking, KYC repositories, and external data sources. Configure BVN/NIN integration for the new platform. Formalise vendor management arrangements with documented SLAs, performance metrics, and escalation procedures. Establish alert SLAs and case management workflows. Conduct a Business Impact Analysis to define RTO/RPO for the AML platform and design disaster recovery arrangements. Document data security architecture covering encryption, access controls, MFA, NDPA compliance, and data sovereignty.
phases.key_deliverables: Integration architecture document covering data flows, API specifications, data formats, error handling, and scalability (approved by IT and MLRO). BVN/NIN integration configuration and testing results. Vendor SLA document covering platform availability, support response times, model validation services, training, and escalation procedures (signed by vendor and institution). Alert SLA document covering timeframes for alert review, investigation, and escalation, differentiated by risk rating (approved by MLRO and Board). Case management workflow document covering case creation, assignment, prioritisation, Maker-Checker controls, escalation paths, and closure procedures (approved by MLRO). Business Impact Analysis report defining RTO/RPO for the AML platform and documenting disaster recovery and business continuity arrangements (approved by IT and MLRO). Data security architecture document covering encryption, access controls, MFA, NDPA compliance, data sovereignty, data retention, and data destruction (approved by IT, MLRO, and Board).
phases.standards_addressed: §5.10 (integration architecture, BVN/NIN integration, scalability). §5.9 (vendor management, alert SLAs, case management workflows, disaster recovery, data security architecture). §5.11 (data security architecture, encryption, MFA, NDPA compliance, data sovereignty, RTO/RPO). §5.2 (BVN/NIN integration for CDD/KYC).

phases.title: System Deployment
phases.objectives: Deploy and configure the integrated AML platform covering transaction monitoring, fraud detection, case management, and regulatory reporting. Integrate the platform with core banking, KYC repositories, and external data sources (BVN/NIN, sanctions lists, adverse media, fraud registries). Configure monitoring rules and scenarios addressing cross-border FX typologies, PEP-related risks, and fraud typologies. Configure sanctions/PEP screening to support continuous screening, adverse media, transaction interdiction, and internal watchlists. Configure case management workflows with Maker-Checker controls and tamper-proof audit trails. Configure regulatory reporting to support automated STR/CTR/FTR generation. Conduct user acceptance testing (UAT) with AML analysts, compliance officers, and MLRO. Deliver initial training to all AML platform users. Go live with transaction monitoring, fraud detection, case management, and regulatory reporting. Establish real-time dashboards for the MLRO, CCO, and senior management.
phases.key_deliverables: AML platform deployment and configuration report documenting platform installation, configuration settings (monitoring rules, scenarios, thresholds, screening lists, case workflows, reporting templates), integration testing results, and UAT sign-off (approved by IT, MLRO, and steering committee). Transaction monitoring rule book documenting all monitoring rules and scenarios, including rule logic, thresholds, expected alert volumes, and rationale (approved by MLRO and Board). Fraud monitoring rule book documenting all fraud rules and scenarios, including rule logic, thresholds, expected alert volumes, and rationale (approved by MLRO and Board). Sanctions/PEP screening configuration document covering screening lists, fuzzy-name matching thresholds, continuous screening frequency, adverse media sources, transaction interdiction logic, and internal watchlist criteria (approved by MLRO). Case management workflow configuration document covering case creation triggers, assignment rules, prioritisation logic, Maker-Checker controls, escalation paths, and closure criteria (approved by MLRO). Regulatory reporting configuration document covering STR/CTR/FTR generation logic, data extraction queries, report templates, submission channels, and audit trail requirements (approved by MLRO). User acceptance testing (UAT) report documenting test scenarios, test results, defects identified, defects resolved, and UAT sign-off by business users (approved by MLRO and steering committee). Training records for all AML platform users (staff name, training date, topics covered, assessment results, trainer identity). Go-live report documenting go-live date, go-live activities, post-go-live monitoring results, and any issues identified and resolved (approved by MLRO and steering committee). Real-time dashboards for MLRO, CCO, and senior management covering alert volumes, case volumes, STR volumes, CTR/FTR volumes, false positive rates, case ageing, and high-risk customer counts.
phases.standards_addressed: §5.1 (integrated AML platform deployment covering transaction monitoring, fraud detection, case management, regulatory reporting). §5.5 (transaction monitoring rules and scenarios, integration with KYC, related-party mapping, alert SLAs). §5.6 (fraud monitoring rules and scenarios, integration with AML workflows). §5.7 (case management workflows, Maker-Checker controls, tamper-proof audit trails). §5.8 (automated STR/CTR/FTR generation, internal MI reporting). §5.3 (continuous sanctions/PEP screening, adverse media, transaction interdiction, internal watchlists). §5.10 (integration with core banking, KYC, external data sources). §5.12 (real-time dashboards, configurable workflows). §5.9 (training records, audit trails).

phases.title: Assurance & Examination Readiness
phases.objectives: Conduct independent internal audit of the AML platform and governance framework. Conduct annual independent model validation of transaction monitoring rules, fraud rules, and customer risk assessment models. Build a comprehensive evidence pack for each of the 12 CBN Baseline Standards, documenting compliance status, supporting evidence, and any residual gaps. Establish an annual self-assessment cycle to monitor ongoing compliance with the CBN Baseline Standards. Deliver annual refresher training to all AML/CFT/CPF staff. Prepare for CBN examination by conducting a mock examination and remediating any findings. Achieve full compliance with all 12 CBN Baseline Standards by March 2028.
phases.key_deliverables: Internal audit report covering the AML platform, governance framework, transaction monitoring, fraud detection, case management, regulatory reporting, sanctions/PEP screening, data security, and integration architecture (issued by internal audit, with management response and remediation plan for any findings). Independent model validation report covering transaction monitoring rules, fraud rules, and customer risk assessment models, including validation methodology, back-testing results, false positive/negative analysis, and recommendations for rule tuning (issued by independent validator, with management response and remediation plan). Evidence pack for each of the 12 CBN Baseline Standards (§5.1–§5.12), including: compliance status (Compliant/Gap Identified/Critical Gap), supporting evidence (policies, procedures, system configuration documents, audit trails, training records, vendor contracts, integration architecture documents, security assessments, model validation reports), and remediation plan for any residual gaps (approved by MLRO and Board). Annual self-assessment template and process document covering all 12 CBN Baseline Standards, with documented self-assessment schedule (e.g., quarterly self-assessment by MLRO, annual self-assessment by internal audit), self-assessment methodology, and escalation process for any gaps identified (approved by MLRO and Board). Training records for annual refresher training delivered to all AML/CFT/CPF staff (staff name, training date, topics covered, assessment results, trainer identity). Mock examination report documenting mock examination scope, findings, and remediation actions (issued by internal audit or external consultant, with management response). Final compliance certification signed by the MLRO and CEO confirming that Sunrise MFB has achieved full compliance with all 12 CBN Baseline Standards by March 2028, with supporting evidence pack attached.
phases.standards_addressed: All standards (§5.1–§5.12) are addressed in the internal audit, model validation, evidence pack, and final compliance certification. §5.9 (internal audit, model validation, training records, annual self-assessment cycle). §5.5 (model validation of transaction monitoring rules). §5.6 (model validation of fraud rules). §5.4 (model validation of customer risk assessment models).

support_section.intro_paragraph: OPEX Consulting and RegTech365 provide end-to-end support for Nigerian financial institutions seeking to achieve compliance with the CBN Baseline Standards for Automated AML Solutions. Our services span advisory (gap assessment, roadmap development, vendor selection, implementation programme management), technology (RegTech365 product suite covering transaction monitoring, fraud detection, case management, and training), and assurance (internal audit, model validation, examination readiness). We have deep expertise in Nigerian AML/CFT/CPF regulation, CBN supervisory expectations, and financial crime typologies affecting Nigerian institutions. Our team includes former CBN examiners, AML practitioners, data scientists, and RegTech specialists. We work with DMBs, MFBs, PSPs, IMTOs, MMOs, and other financial institutions across Nigeria to design and implement proportionate, risk-based AML solutions that meet regulatory requirements and enhance financial crime detection capability.
support_section.advisory_intro: OPEX Consulting provides the following advisory services to support Sunrise Microfinance Bank Ltd's compliance journey:

products.name: RegPort
products.function: Automated AML transaction monitoring, sanctions and PEP screening, customer risk assessment, and regulatory reporting
products.standards_addressed: §5.1 (integrated AML platform), §5.3 (sanctions/PEP screening with AI/fuzzy-name matching, continuous screening, adverse media, transaction interdiction, internal watchlists), §5.4 (customer risk assessment with dynamic risk profiling and behavioural analytics), §5.5 (transaction monitoring with predictive analytics, anomaly detection, rule-based monitoring using KYC attributes, related-party mapping, consolidated alert views), §5.8 (automated STR/CTR/FTR generation), §5.10 (integration with core banking and KYC via standards-based APIs)
products.relevance_to_client: RegPort is highly relevant to Sunrise MFB's compliance needs. The institution currently lacks transaction monitoring, which is a Critical Gap under §5.5. RegPort provides risk-based transaction monitoring with configurable rules and scenarios addressing cross-border FX typologies (relevant to Sunrise MFB's FX/remittance products) and PEP-related risks (relevant to Sunrise MFB's PEP exposure). RegPort also enhances the institution's existing sanctions/PEP screening by adding continuous screening, adverse media monitoring, and transaction interdiction — all of which are required under §5.3 and are material for an institution offering cross-border FX products. RegPort's customer risk assessment module supports dynamic risk profiling based on transaction behaviour, which is required under §5.4 and is currently a gap for Sunrise MFB (risk profiles are static, based on onboarding data only). RegPort's regulatory reporting module automates STR/CTR/FTR generation, which is a Critical Gap under §5.8. RegPort integrates with core banking and KYC repositories via standards-based APIs, which is required under §5.10 and is currently a gap for Sunrise MFB. RegPort is designed for Nigerian financial institutions and includes pre-configured rules and scenarios addressing Nigerian ML/TF/PF typologies, CBN regulatory requirements, and NFIU reporting obligations.

products.name: RegGuard
products.function: Real-time fraud monitoring and detection across cards, e-channels, and digital payment flows
products.standards_addressed: §5.6 (automated fraud monitoring in real-time or near-real-time across all channels, updatable fraud rules, unified or tightly integrated AML/fraud workflows with clear segregation, fraud registry interfaces, historical fraud trend analysis)
products.relevance_to_client: RegGuard is highly relevant to Sunrise MFB's compliance needs. The institution currently lacks automated fraud monitoring, which is a Critical Gap under §5.6. Sunrise MFB operates mobile app and USSD channels in addition to its branch network, and offers retail deposits, credit/loans, and FX/remittance products — all of which are fraud-prone. RegGuard provides real-time fraud monitoring across all channels (branch, mobile app, USSD) with configurable fraud rules addressing account takeover, SIM swap, social engineering, loan application fraud, and remittance fraud. RegGuard integrates with RegPort (AML transaction monitoring) to provide unified financial crime detection, with clear segregation of fraud and AML rules as required by §5.6. RegGuard also interfaces with fraud registries to check customers against known fraudsters, and provides historical fraud trend analysis to inform rule tuning and risk assessment. The absence of fraud monitoring creates material operational risk, customer protection risk, and reputational risk for Sunrise MFB, and also undermines the institution's ability to detect predicate offences for money laundering (e.g., proceeds of fraud being layered through the institution's accounts). RegGuard addresses this gap and enhances the institution's overall financial crime detection capability.

products.name: RegComply
products.function: Enterprise case management with Maker-Checker controls, tamper-proof audit trail, governance logs, and internal MI reporting
products.standards_addressed: §5.7 (Enterprise Case Management with automated case creation, assignment, prioritisation, tracking, role-based workflows, Maker-Checker functionality, escalation paths, tamper-proof audit trails, management reporting on case volumes, ageing, and outcomes), §5.9 (comprehensive tamper-proof audit trail of all system and user activities, governance logs, internal MI reporting to CCO, senior management, ECO, and Board)
products.relevance_to_client: RegComply is highly relevant to Sunrise MFB's compliance needs. The institution currently lacks Enterprise Case Management, which is a Critical Gap under §5.7. RegComply provides automated case creation from transaction monitoring alerts (RegPort), sanctions/PEP screening hits (RegPort), fraud alerts (RegGuard), and manual referrals. RegComply supports role-based workflows with automated assignment, prioritisation (based on risk rating and alert type), and tracking. RegComply implements Maker-Checker functionality (investigator creates case, supervisor reviews and approves closure) and escalation paths to the MLRO for high-risk cases and STR decisions. RegComply generates a tamper-proof audit trail capturing all case actions (timestamps, user identity, decisions, rationale, supporting evidence), which is required under §5.7 and §5.9. RegComply also provides management reporting on case volumes, ageing, outcomes, STR generation, and false positive rates, which is required under §5.7 and §5.8. The absence of case management means that Sunrise MFB has no structured process for investigating alerts, documenting investigation outcomes, escalating cases to the MLRO, or generating STRs. This creates material compliance risk and examination risk. RegComply addresses this gap and provides the institution with a robust, auditable case management capability that meets CBN requirements.

products.name: RegLearn
products.function: AML, data protection, governance, and regulatory compliance training for financial institution staff
products.standards_addressed: §5.9 (AML/CFT/CPF staff training programme with documented training records), Section 6 (cross-cutting training obligations)
products.relevance_to_client: RegLearn is highly relevant to Sunrise MFB's compliance needs. The institution currently lacks a documented staff training programme, which is a governance gap under §5.9. RegLearn provides online AML/CFT/CPF training modules tailored to Nigerian financial institutions, covering: AML/CFT/CPF obligations under the MLPPA, TPPA, and CBN AML/CFT/CPF Regulations; ML/TF/PF typologies (including cross-border FX typologies and PEP-related risks, which are relevant to Sunrise MFB); AML system usage (how to review alerts, investigate cases, generate STRs, configure rules); sanctions and PEP screening; transaction monitoring; case investigation; STR generation; data protection under the NDPA; and regulatory updates. RegLearn includes assessments (quizzes, case studies, practical exercises) to validate staff understanding, and provides automated training record management (staff name, training date, topics covered, assessment results, trainer identity) as evidence for CBN examination. RegLearn supports initial training for new hires, annual refresher training for all AML/CFT/CPF staff, and ad-hoc training when new regulations, typologies, or system features are introduced. Training is a cross-cutting obligation under Section 6 of the Circular and is a prerequisite for effective AML control operation. RegLearn addresses this gap and provides the institution with a scalable, cost-effective training solution that meets CBN requirements.

advisory_services: CBN Implementation Roadmap Development — OPEX Consulting can complete the 12-section CBN roadmap on Sunrise MFB's behalf, leveraging this gap assessment report and the institution's input on implementation approach, budget, and timeline. The roadmap will be Board-ready and will address all 12 CBN Baseline Standards with specific actions, timelines, and accountability. Deliverable: CBN roadmap document (Board-approved, ready for submission by 10 June 2026).

AML Governance Framework Design — OPEX Consulting can design and document a comprehensive AML governance framework covering roles, configuration management, change control, model governance, alert SLAs, vendor management, data retention, access rights, and incident handling. The framework will be tailored to Sunrise MFB's risk profile, operational footprint, and organisational structure, and will be Board-ready. Deliverable: AML governance framework document (Board-approved, operationalised in parallel with platform deployment).

Vendor Selection and Procurement Support — OPEX Consulting can support Sunrise MFB's vendor selection process for the integrated AML platform, including: requirements definition (functional and non-functional requirements based on this gap assessment); vendor evaluation (RFP development, vendor shortlisting, proof-of-concept testing, scoring and ranking); contract negotiation (SLA terms, pricing, support arrangements, exit clauses); and vendor onboarding (kick-off, project governance, implementation planning). Deliverable: Vendor selection report and signed vendor contract.

Implementation Programme Management — OPEX Consulting can provide dedicated programme management for the AML platform implementation, including: project planning (work breakdown structure, timeline, resource allocation, risk register); steering committee support (agenda, minutes, decision log, issue escalation); vendor management (SLA monitoring, performance reviews, issue resolution); user acceptance testing (UAT) coordination (test scenario design, UAT execution, defect management, UAT sign-off); training delivery (initial training for AML platform users, train-the-trainer sessions); go-live support (go-live planning, go-live execution, post-go-live monitoring, hypercare); and post-implementation review (lessons learned, benefits realisation, continuous improvement). Deliverable: Programme management services from vendor selection through go-live and stabilisation.

Independent Model Validation — OPEX Consulting can conduct annual independent model validation of transaction monitoring rules, fraud rules, and customer risk assessment models, as required by §5.5, §5.6, and §5.4. The validation will include: back-testing (testing rules against historical data to validate detection capability); false positive/negative analysis (measuring false positive and false negative rates and recommending threshold adjustments); scenario coverage assessment (validating that rules cover all material ML/TF/PF and fraud typologies relevant to Sunrise MFB); and recommendations for rule tuning (optimising rules to improve detection capability and reduce false positives). Deliverable: Independent model validation report (issued annually, with management response and remediation plan).

Internal Audit and Examination Readiness — OPEX Consulting can conduct independent internal audit of the AML platform and governance framework, and can support Sunrise MFB's preparation for CBN examination. Services include: internal audit (audit planning, fieldwork, testing, findings, management response, remediation tracking); evidence pack development (building a comprehensive evidence pack for each of the 12 CBN Baseline Standards, documenting compliance status, supporting evidence, and any residual gaps); mock examination (simulating a CBN examination to identify gaps and test the institution's examination readiness); and remediation support (supporting the institution to remediate any findings from internal audit or mock examination). Deliverable: Internal audit report, evidence pack, mock examination report, and remediation plan.
support_section.next_steps_box: To engage OPEX Consulting / RegTech365 services, Sunrise Microfinance Bank Ltd should: (1) Review this gap assessment report with the MLRO, CCO, senior management, and Board to confirm findings and prioritise actions. (2) Contact OPEX Consulting to discuss roadmap development, governance framework design, vendor selection, and implementation programme management services. Contact: [insert OPEX contact details]. (3) Request a demonstration of RegTech365 RegPort, RegGuard, RegComply, and RegLearn to evaluate fit with Sunrise MFB's requirements. Contact: [insert RegTech365 contact details]. (4) Engage OPEX Consulting to complete the CBN roadmap by 10 June 2026 (8 weeks remaining). (5) Initiate vendor selection for the integrated AML platform in Q2 2026, with target contract signature in Q3 2026. (6) Establish the AML governance framework and obtain Board approval in Q2 2026 (before roadmap submission). (7) Commence AML platform implementation in Q4 2026, with target go-live in Q2 2027. (8) Engage OPEX Consulting for independent model validation and internal audit in 2027–2028 to support examination readiness. OPEX Consulting and RegTech365 are committed to supporting Sunrise MFB's compliance journey and are available to discuss tailored solutions and pricing.
